title: Parallax Compatibility Case (m-case-format)
description: The case envelope, grouped as given / when / then. Identity and routing (`model`, `tags`, `lane`) plus the explicit `shape` discriminator stay top-level; everything else is bucketed into `given` (ambient world-state before the action: `fixtures`, `apply`, `fault`), `when` (the action under test and how the client performs it: `operation` | `writeSequence` | `scenario` | `coherence` | `concurrency` | `boundary` | `attempts` | `write`, plus context `uow` / `at` / `observedInZ` / `equivalentEncodings`), and `then` (everything the case asserts: `statements`, `referenceSql`, `rows`, `graph`, `tableState`, `affectedRows`, `errorClass`, `nativeCode`, `outcome`, `rejectedRule`, `roundTrips`, `tolerance`). Every SQL statement â€” golden or naive â€” is a `{sql, binds}` statement entry (`$defs/statementEntry`): its `sql` is a dialect-keyed map (`postgres` / `mariadb`) at golden locations (`then.statements`, per-step `statements`) and a plain string at naive locations (`given.apply`); `binds` is authored once per statement and defaults to `[]`. A case is one of nine shapes, named by the required top-level `shape`: a `read` case (a queryable `when.operation`, asserting `then.rows` / `then.graph`); a `writeSequence` case (ordered DML under `when.writeSequence`, asserting `then.tableState`); a `scenario` case (m-unit-work â€” ordered operation steps under `when.scenario`, golden SQL per step); a `conflict` case (m-opt-lock â€” a single-attempt optimistic-lock UPDATE asserted by `then.affectedRows`, or an ordered `when.attempts` retry sequence); a `coherence` case (cross-process cache coherence â€” a two-node sequence under `when.coherence`); an `error` case (m-db-error â€” `then.errorClass` + `then.nativeCode`, triggered by top-level `then.statements` or a `when.concurrency` choreography); a `concurrencySuccess` case (m-read-lock behavioral read lock â€” a `when.concurrency` choreography with NO `then.errorClass`); a `boundary` case (m-auto-retry/m-opt-lock bounded automatic retry â€” `when.boundary` ordered actions + `then.outcome`, on the `api-conformance` lane, carrying no golden SQL); or a `rejected` case (m-value-object / m-op-algebra negative validation â€” a schema-valid `when.operation` OR a `when.write` a model-aware validator MUST refuse PRE-SQL, naming the violated normative rule in `then.rejectedRule`, carrying no golden SQL). Every case carries an optional `lane` (`harness` default | `api-conformance`): a `harness`-lane case executes as today, while an `api-conformance`-lane case (every boundary case, plus the read-lock matrix reads) is schema-validated by the harness but satisfied by each language's API Conformance Suite.

oneOf.description: A read case: a queryable operation with expected rows or an expected object graph.
A writeSequence case: ordered DML asserted by the resulting table state (milestone-chaining temporal writes, batched non-temporal writes, m-detach detached insert/update merge-back).
A scenario case (m-unit-work): ordered operation steps with per-step round-trip counts proving the cache/identity contract. Golden SQL lives per step, so `then.statements` is absent.

A conflict case (m-opt-lock): an optimistic-lock UPDATE asserted by the number of rows it affects. Loads the model's fixtures, OPTIONALLY runs an out-of-band `given.apply` (a concurrent transaction's stale-version mutation), then runs the golden UPDATE(s) and asserts the affected-row count. Two forms: a SINGLE attempt (`when.write` + `then.affectedRows` + `then.statements`); or an ordered `when.attempts` retry sequence (each attempt carrying its own statements + affectedRows + write), proving the m-opt-lock retry contract â€” a stale UPDATE affects 0 rows, then a retry that re-reads the fresh version affects 1.

A coherence case (cross-process cache coherence): a two-node operation sequence under `when.coherence`, run against ONE database over TWO connections (node A, node B). Each step declares its node and golden SQL; the case asserts what node B observes after node A's committed write.

An error-classification case (m-db-error): triggers a real DB error and asserts the neutral category it classifies to (`then.errorClass`) plus the per-dialect native code (`then.nativeCode`). A case MUST declare a trigger: top-level `then.statements` (single-connection, uniqueViolation â€” ordered DML whose final statement raises) OR a `when.concurrency` choreography (two-connection, deadlock / lockWaitTimeout â€” barrier-separated rounds).

A concurrency-success case (m-read-lock behavioral read-lock): barrier-separated `when.concurrency` rounds on two held non-autocommit sessions asserting NO error is raised. Every PRESENT step declares an explicit `kind`: a read step (`kind: read`) carries `rows` it MUST return on its HELD session; a write step (`kind: write`) omits it and asserts only that it did not block/raise. The `not: then.errorClass` guard keeps the shape distinct from an error/concurrency case. The nested `rounds.items.properties.{A,B}.required: [kind]` forces a PRESENT success step to declare its kind structurally, without forcing A/B to exist.

A boundary case (m-auto-retry/m-opt-lock bounded automatic retry): a unit-of-work loop-mechanics case the single-connection harness cannot provoke. It carries `lane: api-conformance` and is satisfied by each language's API Conformance Suite; the harness schema-validates it but does not execute it. It declares the portable unit-of-work body (`when.boundary` ordered actions), the portable expected outcome (`then.outcome`), an OPTIONAL injected fault (`given.fault`), and its retry configuration under `when.uow`. It carries no golden SQL â€” `then.statements` is disallowed â€” and `lane` is REQUIRED and pinned to `api-conformance`.

A rejected case (m-value-object / m-op-algebra / m-inheritance negative validation, resolved Q7 + Q3): a model-aware validator MUST refuse the input BEFORE emitting any SQL, and `then.rejectedRule` names the violated normative rule. `when` carries EXACTLY ONE invalid input â€” an `operation` (a SCHEMA-VALID m-op-algebra node that violates a resolver MUST or the m-value-object materialization/navigation contract: a nested path whose first segment is not a declared value object, a nested comparison whose literal type mismatches the declared attribute, a `deepFetch` path or a relationship navigation aimed at a value-object segment, or a find() rooted at a value object), a `write` (a neutral write row whose value-object document omits a required attribute / required nested value object at any depth, or carries a type-mismatched value), OR a `model` (an inline m-inheritance descriptor document whose cross-entity family invariants a model-aware validator MUST reject â€” an unknown/cyclic parent, zero roots or multiple roots, a concrete subtype with no abstract root ancestor, a redeclared strategy, an abstract node with a table, a table-per-hierarchy concrete subtype missing its `tagValue`, a duplicate family `tagValue`, inconsistent shared-table mapping, or a table-per-concrete-subtype family carrying a tag). The inline `model` stays quarantined inside the case (the shared `models/` registry must remain loadable by sibling cases, resolved Q3) and is round-tripped through descriptor serde before semantic validation asserts the rejection. The `oneOf` below, paired with the `propertyNames` enum that forbids other keys, admits EXACTLY ONE of `operation`/`write`/`model`: a `when` carrying more than one, or none, fails validation (the normative "exactly one invalid input" rule). It carries NO golden SQL â€” `then.statements` is disallowed â€” because rejection is asserted pre-SQL, on no dialect. Purely regex-level negatives (an empty path after the value-object name, a bad-cased segment) are the operation schema's job and stay schema-validation unit tests, NOT rejected cases.

allOf.description: A TEMPORAL / bitemporal optimistic conflict CLOSE â€” a single-form conflict whose `when.at` is the close instant (â†’ the new out_z) â€” run under OPTIMISTIC concurrency (`when.uow.concurrency: optimistic`) MUST carry `when.observedInZ`, the observed processing-from (in_z) the close gates on (`and in_z = ?`). A VERSIONED conflict carries no `when.at`, so this never fires on it; a LOCKING-mode temporal close is ungated and omits `observedInZ`. (The retry form carries `at` / `observedInZ` per attempt; the reference-harness gate + generating adapter enforce the per-attempt token, which a subschema cannot condition on the root `uow`.)

model.description: Path to the model descriptor, relative to core/compatibility/.

tags.description: Module/feature tags driving coverage and test selection.

lane.description: Which executor satisfies this case (m-case-format lane routing). `harness` (the default) â€” the m-case-format compatibility harness executes it as today (golden-SQL / data observables). `api-conformance` â€” a runtime-loop or read-lock-matrix branch a single-connection harness cannot provoke: the harness schema-validates it but does NOT execute it, and each language's API Conformance Suite MUST satisfy it. Every `boundary`-shape case is `api-conformance` by construction; the read-lock matrix reads (`m-read-lock-002`-`m-read-lock-005`) are `read`-shape `api-conformance` cases.

shape.description: The explicit case-shape discriminator: cases are self-describing, and the `oneOf` keys on this `const` for precise validation errors. The reference harness and the TypeScript conformance loader read this field directly instead of sniffing which keys happen to be present. The schema still enforces shape-consistent required members (per branch), so a mislabeled case cannot validate.

given.description: Ambient world-state established BEFORE the action under test: the model fixtures to load, out-of-band SQL to apply verbatim, and any injected fault. Optional â€” a case that starts from the model's default fixtures and injects nothing omits `given` entirely.

fixtures.description: Whether the model's fixtures are loaded BEFORE the action â€” so a sequence can mutate pre-existing persisted rows (the m-detach detached-update merge-back case; the m-read-lock held-session reads). Defaults to false: a writeSequence case starts from an empty schema and builds its own state (the m-audit-write milestone-chaining and m-batch-write batched-insert cases).

apply.description: conflict / temporal-conflict cases (m-opt-lock) only. An ordered list of out-of-band naive statement entries the harness applies verbatim AFTER the fixtures load and BEFORE the golden UPDATE, simulating a concurrent transaction that mutated the row (e.g. bumping the optimistic-lock version) so the golden UPDATE's stale-version predicate now matches zero rows. Each entry is a `{sql, binds}` statement whose `sql` is a plain string (dialect-agnostic naive SQL, run on every dialect). Absent for the success case (the version is fresh, so the UPDATE affects one row).

fault.description: boundary cases only. A portable fault kind injected at the database-port seam to drive the retry loop (aligned with the m-db-error `errorClass` vocabulary): a `serialization-failure` or `deadlock` transient (always retriable), a `lock-wait-timeout` (never retriable), or an `optimistic-lock-conflict` (retriable only under `when.uow.retryOptimisticConflicts`). Absent when the case exercises only loop configuration (`retries: 0`, the callback-withheld-on-abort case).

when.description: The action under test and how the client performs it. Exactly one action member is present per shape (`operation` | `writeSequence` | `scenario` | `coherence` | `concurrency` | `boundary` | `attempts`, plus the single-attempt conflict's `write`); the context members (`uow`, `at`, `observedInZ`, `equivalentEncodings`) describe the unit-of-work mode, transaction instant, observed version, and alternate surface encodings.

uow.description: The unit-of-work configuration the action runs under (m-unit-work strategy selection + m-auto-retry/m-opt-lock bounded automatic retry). Optional and descriptive: it declares the per-unit-of-work concurrency mode (so a case's mode-dependent golden SQL is self-describing) and the retry configuration a boundary case exercises. `concurrency`: `locking` â€” the default m-read-lock implicit shared read lock, keyed updates advance the version with NO gate; `optimistic` â€” reads take no lock and every keyed update / temporal close GATES on the observed version. The harness executes the authored golden SQL either way; the block documents WHICH mode produced it.

concurrency.description: The correctness strategy this unit of work selects: `locking` (default â€” m-read-lock implicit shared read lock; version advances but no gate is emitted) or `optimistic` (m-opt-lock â€” reads take no lock; keyed updates and temporal closes gate on the observed version).

retries.description: The bound on automatic re-executions of the unit-of-work body after a retriable failure (m-auto-retry/m-opt-lock bounded automatic retry). Default 10; `0` disables the loop, so even a transient failure surfaces to the caller.

retryOptimisticConflicts.description: Whether an optimistic-lock conflict joins the retriable set for this unit of work (m-opt-lock). Default false â€” a conflict surfaces to the caller after one attempt. Transient database failures (deadlock / serialization failure) are always retriable regardless of this flag.

operation.description: A canonical m-op-algebra algebra node (validated separately against operation.schema.json). Present on read cases; absent on writeSequence cases.

model.description: rejected cases only (m-inheritance, resolved Q3). An INLINE model descriptor document (an instance of metamodel.schema.json) carrying an invalid inheritance family a model-aware semantic validator MUST reject before any SQL â€” the cross-entity family invariants (parent resolution / acyclicity / exactly one root / concrete-under-abstract-root / tag placement / tag presence under table-per-hierarchy / family-wide tagValue uniqueness / shared-table consistency) that per-entity schema validation cannot catch. Kept inline (never in the shared `models/` registry) so an invalid family cannot break sibling cases that load real models; round-tripped through descriptor serde before semantic validation asserts `then.rejectedRule`.

targetEntity.description: read cases only. The entity a read TARGETS â€” the queried position `when.operation` starts from (m-case-format, resolved Q1). It names the whole family for an abstract root, its concrete descendants for an abstract subtype, and itself for a concrete subtype; today every entity is concrete, so it names exactly the entity whose rows the read returns. REQUIRED on every read case, so the read side reaches the same explicit-entity standard the write side already meets with `writeSequence[].entity` (an `all: {}` read no longer names its subject only in a comment or the golden SQL). A model-aware harness cross-checks it against every queried-entity `Class.attribute` / `Class.relationship` reference in the operation (until inheritance families exist, 'consistent' means 'equal').

equivalentEncodings.description: Optional alternate surface encodings of the same operation (e.g. a prefix vs a fluent spelling, or differently-ordered keys). Each MUST canonicalize to `when.operation` via the serde seam; the harness asserts this dialect-agnostically (layer 4c), proving precedence/serialization fidelity in the fixture itself.

writeSequence.description: A writeSequence case: an ordered list of write steps the golden DML (`then.statements`) realizes. Each step names a mutation kind on an entity; the golden SQL is the matching ordered list of DML statements (one or more per step), applied in order, after which the harness asserts `then.tableState`. Used by milestone-chaining temporal writes (m-audit-write), batched non-temporal writes (m-batch-write), detached merge-back delete (m-detach), and the minimal dependent cascade-delete witness (m-cascade-delete/m-unit-work).

allOf.description: A bitemporal `*Until` step (the rectangle split) bounds a mutation to a business valid-time window: it MUST carry both `at` (the transaction instant â†’ in_z) and `until` (the business window end â†’ thru_z).

A milestone-close `terminate` step MUST carry its close instant â€” `at` (the processing axis â†’ out_z) for an audit-only entity, or `businessAt` (the business axis â†’ thru_z) for a business-only one.

mutation.description: The mutation kind. insert / update / terminate are the m-audit-write audit-only mutation surface and also cover the m-batch-write batched non-temporal writes; delete covers ordinary non-temporal delete / detached-delete merge-back; cascadeDelete is the minimal dependent-delete witness over m-descriptor dependent relationships; insertUntil / updateUntil / terminateUntil are the full-bitemporal `*Until` trio that bound a mutation to a business window.

entity.description: The temporal entity the mutation targets.

statements.description: How many golden DML statements this step emits (for documentation / count consistency). The sum over all steps MUST equal the number of `then.statements` per dialect. This is an integer COUNT, not a statement-entry list (a writeSequence step carries no per-step SQL â€” the golden DML lives at `then.statements`).

rows.description: The neutral write input (â‘ ) for this step: the row(s) the mutation operates on, as flat attribute-named literals (`$defs/writeRow`). A GENERATING adapter classifies each field by metamodel role and derives the emitted column list + ORDER from `columnOrder(entity)`, plus the SQL and the binds. `then.statements` stays an independent oracle the derivation cross-checks against. REQUIRED on every writeSequence step.

at.description: The transaction / processing instant a TEMPORAL (audit-only) write records â€” the milestone's `in_z`. A GENERATING adapter DERIVES the bookkeeping from it (open `in_z = at`, `out_z = infinity`; close `out_z = at`). Present on a processing-axis temporal step; absent on a non-temporal batched write (and replaced by `businessAt` on a business-only step). Required for a `*Until` step and for a `terminate` close.

until.description: The bitemporal valid-time end bound a `*Until` write closes the business window at (consumed by the full-bitemporal rectangle-split cases) â€” the business analogue of `at`. Absent on an audit-only step.

businessAt.description: The BUSINESS-axis instant a business-temporal milestone-chaining write records â€” the milestone's `from_z` (and, on a close, the prior row's `thru_z`). Present on a business-only temporal step; a processing (audit-only) step carries `at` instead. Required for a business-only `terminate` close.

note.description: Optional human-readable description of the milestone-chaining effect.

scenario.description: A scenario (m-unit-work) case: an ordered list of steps proving the unit-of-work / identity / query-cache contract. A step is either a READ step (issues a `find`) or a WRITE step (commits DML via `write`). Golden SQL lives PER STEP (as `statements`), not at `then.statements`: each find costs a declared number of database round trips, and an interleaved committed write lets a later step prove read-your-own-writes or query-cache invalidation. The harness asserts the round-trip counts are internally consistent with the golden SQL each step lists.

oneOf.description: A read step: issues a find. A cache miss costs >= 1 round trip and lists golden SQL; a cache hit costs 0 and lists none. It names its queried entity with `targetEntity` (m-case-format, resolved Q1), exactly as a read case does.

A write step: applies golden DML between finds. By default the DML is COMMITTED so a later find observes it; with `rollback: true` the DML is applied and then ROLLED BACK. A no-op write issues NO DML: it declares `roundTrips: 0` and lists no `statements`.

An ACTION step (m-case-format lifecycle vocabulary, COR-30): performs a managed-object lifecycle verb named by `action` against the result of an earlier step (`on`). It carries per-step golden SQL (`statements`) when the verb hits the database (a `load` / `access` relationship query, a `flush` / `mergeBack` / `commit` DML) exactly as a read / write step does, and per-step observables: `expectRows` (rows a `load` returns or a re-read observes), `expectState` (the object's lifecycle state after the verb), `expectError` (a neutral application-lifecycle error the verb raises), and `sameObjectAs` / `differentObjectFrom` reference-identity assertions. A cache-hit re-access lists no golden SQL and declares `roundTrips: 0`, exactly like a cache-hit read step. Golden SQL still lives PER STEP, so a scenario case with action steps carries no top-level `then.statements`.

allOf.description: `set` is a MUTATE-only attribute-assignment map (the new attribute values a `mutate` action assigns). A step carrying `set` MUST be a `mutate` action step â€” `set` on a read / write step, or on a non-`mutate` action verb, is invalid.

A single step declares AT MOST ONE of `sameObjectAs` / `differentObjectFrom`: a step's identity relationship to one anchor is either sameness OR difference, never both (in particular, they can never point at the SAME step). Distinct identity anchors are expressed by separate steps or by the adapter's per-position identity checks (m-conformance-adapter).

An OBJECT-TARGETING action verb (`mutate` / `detachCopy` / `load` / `access` / `mergeBack`) acts on a PRIOR step's result, so it MUST name that source with `on`. The BOUNDARY / unit-of-work verbs (`flush` / `commit` / `abort`) operate on the unit of work as a whole rather than a specific prior object, so `on` is OPTIONAL for them (a `flush` MAY still carry `on` to document the buffered write it materializes). This is a per-verb conditional, NOT a blanket `on` requirement â€” a blanket rule would wrongly reject a legitimate boundary step.
A scenario-step `referenceSql` is a naive oracle for a read (`find`) only; writes and lifecycle actions do not produce rows to compare.

find.description: The operation a READ step issues (a canonical m-op-algebra algebra node). Validated against operation.schema.json by the harness. Present on read steps; absent on write steps.

targetEntity.description: read steps only. The entity this step's `find` targets â€” the queried position it starts from (m-case-format, resolved Q1). REQUIRED on every read step and absent on write steps, mirroring a read case's `when.targetEntity`.

write.description: Marks a WRITE step. Existing cases retain their legacy string label; predicate-selected writes carry the neutral `$defs/predicateWrite` instruction that compile/run and API no-drift checks consume independently of golden SQL.

rollback.description: Optional (write steps only). When true, the step's golden DML is applied and then ROLLED BACK instead of committed â€” the observable form of the m-unit-work abort contract. The rolled-back DML still executes, so the step's roundTrips count its statements exactly as a committed write does.

roundTrips.description: How many database round trips this step costs. A READ cache HIT costs 0; a read cache MISS that executes one statement costs 1. A WRITE step's round trips equal the committed DML statements it lists. MUST equal the number of golden SQL statements this step lists for each dialect.

statements.description: The golden SQL this step emits, as an ordered list of `{sql, binds}` statement entries (dialect-keyed map form). Present only for steps that hit the database (roundTrips >= 1); absent for a cache hit (roundTrips 0).

expectRows.description: The rows this step's find must return against the fixture data. A cache-hit step that reuses an earlier step's result MAY restate the same expected rows.

referenceSql.description: A read step's independent naive SQL oracle. It has the same string-or-dialect-map shape as `then.referenceSql` and, like that oracle, is self-contained rather than reusing golden binds.

sameObjectAs.description: Zero-based index of an earlier scenario step whose result denotes the SAME logical object(s) as this step (the identity-cache contract: same primary key => same object). Legal on a read step or an action step (a `load` / `access` re-read denoting the same managed object as an earlier step).

differentObjectFrom.description: Zero-based index of an earlier scenario step whose result denotes a DISTINCT object from this step â€” the reference-INEQUALITY counterpart of `sameObjectAs` (m-identity-map: two finite coordinates in one milestone are distinct pinned views even when their row values are identical, so value equality cannot distinguish them). Graded as REFERENCE identity by each language's API Conformance Suite; the wire harness proves the accompanying SQL / rows and skips this observable.

action.description: Marks an ACTION step and names the managed-object lifecycle verb it performs (m-case-format lifecycle vocabulary, COR-30). `mutate` â€” assign the attributes in `set` in memory (no SQL for a snapshot / detached object). `detachCopy` â€” take a detached deep copy of the target (m-detach). `load` â€” explicitly trigger a deferred relationship load (m-deep-fetch), the portable, mandatory load trigger. `access` â€” read an already-loaded relationship / operation-backed list (m-op-list; no SQL when already populated). `flush` â€” emit the unit of work's buffered DML. `mergeBack` â€” reconcile a detached copy with the store (m-detach). `commit` / `abort` â€” end the unit of work, committing or discarding it (m-unit-work / m-detach).

on.description: The earlier scenario step(s) whose result this action targets â€” the source object(s) the verb operates on. A single zero-based index, or an array of indices when the action spans sources at DIFFERENT lowered coordinates (a coordinate-grouped `load`, m-deep-fetch / m-identity-map). Every index MUST name an EARLIER step (the harness asserts `0 <= index < the current step's index`); the array form's indices MUST be UNIQUE (`uniqueItems`) â€” a source is referenced at most once. REQUIRED for object-targeting verbs and optional for boundary verbs (see the sibling `allOf`).

set.description: mutate action only (enforced by the sibling `allOf`). The attribute assignments a `mutate` applies, as an attribute-name-keyed map of new values.

path.description: load / access action only. The relationship (or dotted multi-hop relationship path) the verb navigates from the target object â€” e.g. `items` or `items.statuses`.

expectState.description: The lifecycle state the target object is in after this step (m-detach five-state machine): `in-memory` (new, uninserted), `persisted` (DB-backed, interned), `deleted` (marked for delete), `detached` (deep copy / scope-ended), or `detached-deleted` (detached copy marked for delete). An adapter-delegated observable â€” graded by each language's API Conformance Suite via the m-conformance-adapter `stateChecks` observation, and skipped by the wire harness. Legal on a read step or an action step.

expectError.description: A neutral application-lifecycle error this step's verb MUST raise, from a closed vocabulary defined where each error is defined: `detached-relationship-load` (a deferred relationship load on a detached object â€” m-detach) or `processing-pin-read-only` (a mutation through a finite processing-axis pinned view â€” m-identity-map). Distinct from the m-db-error DB-error taxonomy. An adapter-delegated observable â€” graded by each language's API Conformance Suite via the m-conformance-adapter `errors` observation, and skipped by the wire harness.

identityAttr.description: The result-column name carrying the primary-key identity, used to check `sameObjectAs`. Defaults to the queried entity's primary-key column.

note.description: Optional human-readable description of the step's role in the scenario.

coherence.description: A coherence (cross-process cache coherence) case: an ordered list of steps run against ONE database over TWO connections modeling two application servers (node A, node B). Each step names the node it runs on, the operation it issues, and its golden SQL (`statements`); a write step (kind `write`) COMMITS on its node, a read step (kind `read`) is a query. The final step is node B's re-fetch after node A's committed write â€” it carries `observeRows`, the post-write rows node B MUST observe.

allOf.description: A read step (`kind: read`) issues a `find` and MUST name its queried entity with `targetEntity` (m-case-format, resolved Q1), exactly as a read case does.

node.description: Which application-server node (which connection) this step runs on. `A` is the writer; `B` is the peer whose cache must converge on A's committed state.

kind.description: `read` issues a query (a seed read or a re-fetch); `write` issues DML that COMMITS on its node (the change a peer must observe).

find.description: The operation a `read` step issues (a canonical m-op-algebra algebra node, validated against operation.schema.json). Present on read steps; absent on write steps.

targetEntity.description: read steps only. The entity this step's `find` targets â€” the queried position it starts from (m-case-format, resolved Q1). REQUIRED on every `read` step and absent on `write` steps, mirroring a read case's `when.targetEntity`.

statements.description: The golden SQL this step emits, as an ordered list of `{sql, binds}` statement entries (dialect-keyed map form). A read step's is a SELECT; a write step's is the committing DML.

observeRows.description: The rows this step MUST observe. On the final node-B re-fetch this is the POST-WRITE state (proving the cache converged on node A's committed write); on a seed read it is the pre-write state.

sameObjectAs.description: Zero-based index of an earlier coherence step (on the SAME node) whose observed rows denote the SAME logical object(s) as this step â€” the identity-preservation contract: the cross-process refresh updates the interned object in place (same primary key => same object).

identityAttr.description: The result-column name carrying the primary-key identity, used to check `sameObjectAs`. Defaults to the model root entity's primary-key column.

note.description: Optional human-readable description of the step's role in the coherence scenario.

concurrency.description: The two-connection choreography for deadlock / lockWaitTimeout error cases and the concurrency-success read-lock cases. Ordered, barrier-separated rounds; each round names the statements nodes A and B run that round (a node absent from a round is idle). Each node step carries `statements` (dialect-keyed golden SQL entries), mirroring a coherence step. The runner runs each node on its own non-autocommit session, synchronizes rounds with a barrier, and (for an error case) classifies the error raised in the contention round.

boundary.description: boundary cases only (m-auto-retry/m-opt-lock bounded automatic retry). An ordered list of the actions the unit-of-work body performs, portably described so the case is self-documenting without carrying golden SQL. The m-case-format harness never executes these (the case is `api-conformance`-lane); each language's suite realizes them through its idiomatic public API.

action.description: The unit-of-work action this step performs, portably named (the concrete API is per-language).

note.description: Optional human-readable description of the action's role in the loop scenario.

attempts.description: conflict cases (m-opt-lock), retry form. An ordered list of optimistic-lock UPDATE attempts applied (after any `given.apply`) in sequence, each asserting its own affected-row count â€” proving the m-opt-lock retry contract end-to-end: the first (stale-version) attempt affects 0 rows, then a retry that re-reads the now-fresh version and re-applies affects 1. When present, the single-attempt `then.statements` / `then.affectedRows` are absent and `then.tableState` (when authored) asserts the retried write landed.

statements.description: This attempt's golden UPDATE, as an ordered list of `{sql, binds}` statement entries (dialect-keyed map form).

write.description: This attempt's neutral write input (â‘ ): the flat attribute-named row (`$defs/writeRow`) the attempt operates on. For a VERSIONED conflict its reserved `observedVersion` is the version THIS attempt gates on; for a TEMPORAL close it carries the milestone pk (+ a bitemporal business discriminator), with the close instant and observed gate in this attempt's `at` / `observedInZ`. REQUIRED on every retry attempt.

at.description: conflict RETRY cases (m-opt-lock), temporal-close form. THIS attempt's close instant (â†’ the new `out_z` the milestone-closing UPDATE sets).

observedInZ.description: conflict RETRY cases (m-opt-lock), temporal-close form. THIS attempt's observed processing-from (`in_z`) â€” the optimistic gate the attempt binds (`and in_z = ?`). Absent in locking mode.

affectedRows.description: The rows this attempt's UPDATE must affect: 0 for the stale-version attempt (conflict), 1 for the fresh-version retry (success). Named to match the single-attempt `then.affectedRows` â€” no legacy `expected*` vocabulary survives in a migrated case body.

note.description: Optional human-readable description of this attempt's role in the retry sequence.

write.description: conflict cases (m-opt-lock), single-attempt form. The neutral write input (â‘ ) for this conflict's versioned UPDATE (or a temporal / bitemporal close), as a flat attribute-named literal (`$defs/writeRow`): the primary-key attribute (classified to the `where` key), every assigned domain attribute (the `set` columns), and the reserved `observedVersion` control key. A TEMPORAL / bitemporal close carries only the milestone pk (+ a bitemporal business discriminator); its close instant and observed gate ride in `when.at` / `when.observedInZ`. Either way the golden UPDATE + binds become an INDEPENDENT cross-check rather than a golden-parse tautology.

at.description: conflict cases (m-opt-lock), temporal-close form. The close instant a TEMPORAL / bitemporal optimistic conflict close records â€” the new `out_z` the milestone-closing UPDATE sets, keyed on the still-open current row. A GENERATING adapter DERIVES the close binds from it. Present on a temporal-conflict close; absent on a versioned conflict.

observedInZ.description: conflict cases (m-opt-lock), temporal-close form. The processing-from (`in_z`) the unit of work observed â€” the optimistic-lock version analogue a TEMPORAL / bitemporal entity gates on. In optimistic mode the close gains the `and in_z = ?` gate bound to this value; ABSENT in locking mode. It is the temporal analogue of a versioned write's `observedVersion`.

then.description: Everything the case asserts after the action runs: the golden SQL an implementation is expected to emit (`statements`), the naive oracle (`referenceSql`), the observed data (`rows` / `graph` / `tableState`), the counts and codes (`affectedRows` / `errorClass` / `nativeCode` / `roundTrips`), the portable boundary `outcome`, and the numeric-comparison `tolerance`.

statements.description: The golden SQL an implementation is expected to emit, as an ordered list of `{sql, binds}` statement entries. Each entry's `sql` is a dialect-keyed map (`postgres` / `mariadb`) and its `binds` are authored once (dialect-agnostic), defaulting to `[]`. For a deep-fetch read the list has one entry per relationship/deep-fetch level; for a writeSequence one entry per DML statement. The per-level binds for a deep-fetch child level are the distinct parent keys gathered from the previous level, which the harness asserts equal the authored binds.

rows.description: The rows the query must return against the fixture data (single-statement / flat-result cases). An ABSTRACT-target inheritance read (m-inheritance, resolved Q6) additionally carries a `familyVariant` key per row â€” the CONCRETE subtype name of that row, materialized from the tag metadata map (`tagValue` -> subtype name), NOT projected as SQL â€” alongside the full concrete-superset columns (non-applicable subtype columns are null). Row objects stay open, so `familyVariant` needs no separate schema property.

graph.description: The assembled object graph a read must produce. For a deep fetch: the root rows, each carrying its eager-fetched related rows under the relationship name â€” OR, for a NARROWED polymorphic hop (m-deep-fetch, m-inheritance), under the DERIVED narrowed view key `<rel>[<Concrete>,<Concrete>]` (the local relationship name, the effective concrete-subtype set in canonical alphabetical order by entity name, no spaces; equivalent authored narrowings collapse to the same key). A polymorphic view's child objects additionally carry `familyVariant` (the concrete subtype name, materialized from the tag map, never projected as SQL). For a value-object materialization read (m-value-object): the owning entity's rows, each carrying its embedded value-object values decoded from the single structured-document column under the value-object name (a nested object for a `one` member, an ordered list for a `many` member, to arbitrary depth). Keyed by the root class name, whose value is a list of root objects; a related set appears under its relationship name (or narrowed view key), and a value-object member under its declared name. When a relationship reaches an ANCESTOR node already on the current path (a true BACK-REFERENCE cycle, m-snapshot-read), recursion stops and the cycle point carries a PK-ONLY stub â€” ONLY the referenced node's primary-key attribute(s), no other scalars, no relationships; a diamond-shared node at a NON-cyclic position keeps its full-value representation (the stub is scoped to true cycles). The stub proves nothing about sameness by itself â€” the cycle's same-node claim rides `then.identityChecks`.

graphs.description: An ORDERED array of per-milestone snapshot graphs a `history` / `asOfRange` read materializes (m-snapshot-read, resolved Q5a), coexisting with the single-graph `then.graph` exactly as `then.rows` does: a single-instant read carries `graph`, a milestone-set read carries `graphs`. Each entry pairs a milestone `pin` â€” the EDGE coordinate this graph is pinned at, its OWN milestone's from-instant per as-of axis, NOT a shared root pin â€” with the assembled `graph` at that pin. So `history` returns one independently edge-pinned graph per milestone and `asOfRange` one per overlapping milestone (m-temporal-read edge-point reads). Each `graph` has the same root-class-keyed shape as `then.graph`.

pin.description: The milestone edge coordinate this graph is pinned at, keyed by the as-of ATTRIBUTE name (`processingDate` / `businessDate`) whose value is the milestone's own from-instant (ISO-8601 UTC). Each graph in the array carries its OWN pin â€” edge-pinning, so a milestone-set read yields independently-pinned graphs, one per milestone, never a shared root pin.

graph.description: The assembled object graph at this milestone's pin â€” the same root-class-keyed shape as `then.graph`.

identityChecks.description: Declared reference-identity expectations over graph node positions (m-snapshot-read, resolved Q5b), mirroring the m-conformance-adapter `identityCheck`. Each entry is `{left, right, same}`: `left` / `right` are JSON Pointers into the case (into `then.graph` / `then.graphs`) naming the two compared node positions, and `same` is the asserted reference verdict. Authored where the graph JSON cannot express identity by value â€” the BACK-REFERENCE cycle (m-snapshot-read-011), whose PK-only stub proves nothing about sameness by itself: the cycle's same-node claim (`items[0].order` is the SAME node as the root, not a lookalike copy) rides this observation, graded as REFERENCE identity by each language's API Conformance Suite while the wire harness proves the accompanying golden SQL / graph. An adapter-delegated observable â€” the wire harness validates it is well-formed and skips grading it.

tableState.description: The resulting table state a writeSequence or conflict case asserts after applying its ordered DML golden SQL. Keyed by table name; each value is the full set of rows that table must contain (order-insensitive). Row values speak DB column names; timestamp columns are authored as ISO-8601 UTC strings at core microsecond precision, with the open-bound `infinity` as the literal string `infinity`.

affectedRows.description: conflict cases (m-opt-lock), single-attempt form. The number of rows the golden UPDATE must affect after any `given.apply` is applied. A stale optimistic-lock version => 0 (conflict detected); a fresh version => 1 (success). This is the observable form of optimistic-lock conflict detection: `updatedRows != 1` is the conflict signal.

errorClass.description: error cases (m-db-error). The neutral m-db-error error category a triggered DB error MUST classify to. uniqueViolation = duplicate-key; deadlock = deadlock or serialization failure (retriable); lockWaitTimeout = blocked past the lock-wait budget.

nativeCode.description: error cases (m-db-error). The native code each dialect's driver MUST surface for this error, witnessing the divergence the classifier absorbs: Postgres a SQLSTATE string (e.g. "23505"), MariaDB a vendor errno (e.g. 1062). `errorClass` is the single dialect-neutral seam output; this is the per-dialect input.

outcome.description: boundary cases only. The portable expected outcome: `committed` (the unit of work eventually commits â€” the injected fault was auto-retried away), `aborted` (the closure threw and the boundary rolled back, withholding the callback's return value), or a surfaced error kind the caller observes (`optimistic-lock-conflict` when a conflict is not opted into retry; `deadlock` / `serialization-failure` / `lock-wait-timeout` when a transient exhausts the bound or the loop is disabled). The concrete per-language error type stays language-local; this is the neutral kind the suite asserts.

rejectedRule.description: rejected cases (m-value-object / m-op-algebra / m-inheritance negative validation, resolved Q7 + Q3/Q4). The normative rule the input violates, from a small closed vocabulary a model-aware PRE-SQL validator (and every language implementation) MUST enforce, asserted BEFORE any SQL is emitted. OPERATION rules: `nested-path-first-segment-not-value-object` (a nested path's first segment names no declared value object on the queried entity â€” m-op-algebra nested-predicate resolver MUST); `nested-path-unknown-member` (an intermediate segment names no declared nested value object, or a leaf names no declared attribute); `nested-literal-type-mismatch` (a nested comparison/membership literal's type differs from the leaf attribute's declared neutral type â€” m-op-algebra typed-literal MUST); `deep-fetch-value-object-segment` (a `deepFetch` path segment names a value object â€” m-value-object materialization/navigation contract 4, m-deep-fetch); `navigate-value-object-target` (a `navigate`/`exists`/`notExists` targets a value object â€” m-value-object contract 4, m-navigate); `find-root-value-object` (a find() is rooted at a value object â€” m-value-object contract 5). WRITE rules: `write-required-attribute-missing` (a required `nullable:false` attribute is absent at some depth â€” m-value-object write validation); `write-required-value-object-missing` (a required `nullable:false` nested value object is absent, or a required `many` array is absent â€” emptiness is fine); `write-value-type-mismatch` (a document field value's type differs from the declared attribute's neutral type). SUBTYPE-WRITE rules (m-inheritance x concrete-subtype writes, Phase 7 â€” a schema-valid neutral write input a model-aware validator MUST refuse pre-SQL because it violates the concrete-subtype write protocol): `subtype-write-set-based-unsupported` (a keyless / predicate-driven write to an inheritance family â€” a per-object concrete-subtype write is KEYED, so a payload carrying no primary-key attribute denotes an unsupported set-based inheritance write, out of scope for this slice); `subtype-write-metadata-field` (a payload carries a FRAMEWORK-OWNED metadata field â€” the tag column, `tag`, `tagValue`, or `familyVariant` â€” which a concrete-subtype write DERIVES from the subtype's tagValue and never accepts as input); `subtype-write-sibling-attribute` (a payload carries an attribute declared on a SIBLING / unrelated concrete branch, so no single concrete subtype in the target's effective set accepts every authored field â€” the accepted fields are exactly the target's ancestry chain: root + abstract ancestors + own); `abstract-write-target` (a create / update / delete / terminate handle aimed at an ABSTRACT root or abstract subtype â€” writes are concrete-subtype only). MODEL rules (m-inheritance closed-tree family invariants, one per invariant per resolved Q4): `inheritance-unknown-parent` (a `parent` names no entity in the descriptor); `inheritance-cycle` (parent links form a cycle); `inheritance-missing-root` (the descriptor declares inheritance participants but NO root â€” a zero-root/abstract-orphan family; distinct from `inheritance-multiple-roots`, which is strictly more than one); `inheritance-multiple-roots` (a family reaches strictly MORE THAN ONE root, or the descriptor declares two roots for one tree; a family has exactly one root, so both zero and more-than-one are rejected, by `inheritance-missing-root` and this rule respectively); `inheritance-concrete-without-abstract-root` (a concrete subtype whose ancestry has no abstract root); `inheritance-abstract-node-with-table` (a `root` / `abstract-subtype` declares a physical table â€” also caught by the entity `table` conditional); `inheritance-abstract-node-fixture-rows` (fixture rows are keyed to an abstract root / abstract subtype â€” enforced at fixture load); `inheritance-strategy-redeclared` (a non-root participant redeclares `strategy`); `inheritance-missing-tag-value` (a table-per-hierarchy concrete subtype declares no `tagValue` â€” the shared table cannot discriminate its rows without one; the per-entity schema leaves `tagValue` optional and delegates its presence-under-table-per-hierarchy to this semantic rule); `inheritance-duplicate-tag-value` (two concrete subtypes in one table-per-hierarchy family share a `tagValue`); `inheritance-inconsistent-hierarchy-table` (table-per-hierarchy concrete subtypes map to different physical tables); `inheritance-tag-on-concrete-subtype-strategy` (a table-per-concrete-subtype family declares a `tag` or `tagValue`). NARROW / SUBTYPE-SCOPE operation rules (m-op-algebra Ă— m-inheritance â€” a schema-valid operation a model-aware validator MUST refuse pre-SQL): `narrow-outside-position` (a `narrow` node's resolved effective concrete-subtype set is not a SUBSET of the ACTIVE polymorphic position â€” the position threaded into the node, the read's `targetEntity` or the enclosing narrow's resolved set, CLAMPED to (intersected with) the position the node's `entity` names â€” so narrowing broadens beyond the position in scope, e.g. narrowing to a concrete outside the active set, or a nested narrow broadening back out of the set the enclosing narrow established); `narrow-empty-effective-set` (a `narrow`'s authored `to` list resolves to the EMPTY concrete-subtype set); `subtype-attribute-outside-narrow-scope` (a predicate references a concrete-subtype-declared attribute at a polymorphic position that is not narrowed to that subtype, so the attribute is not available to every concrete in the effective set); `narrow-outside-relationship-target` (a `narrow` in a navigation filter's `op`, or a deep-fetch path segment's `narrow`, either names an `entity` that is not the RELATIONSHIP TARGET exactly â€” a relationship-scope narrow MUST set `entity` to the target and reach subtypes via `to`, never by naming a broader or other position â€” or resolves its `to` set to a concrete-subtype set that is NOT a SUBSET of the RELATIONSHIP TARGET's effective concrete set; narrowing a polymorphic relationship to a concrete outside its reachable set, even one sharing the broader family root, is rejected; m-navigate / m-deep-fetch, resolved Q10).

roundTrips.description: Declared number of SQL statements for the operation. For a deep-fetch read case this MUST equal the number of authored/executed `then.statements` for each dialect; child SQL is omitted when an earlier level gathers no parent keys. For a writeSequence case it MUST equal the number of ordered DML statements; for a scenario case it MUST equal the SUM of the steps' per-step roundTrips.

tolerance.description: Absolute tolerance for numeric row comparison. Omit for exact comparison (the default; money/counts/exact aggregates compare exactly in Decimal space). Declare ONLY for inherently inexact results (stddev/variance, repeating-decimal avg) that cannot be authored exactly and differ in scale across dialects; comparison then becomes abs(actual - expected) <= tolerance.

jsonPointer.description: A JSON Pointer into the case (RFC 6901), naming a graph node position for a `then.identityChecks` entry. Mirrors the m-conformance-adapter `jsonPointer` def: the empty string (whole document) or a `/`-rooted path.

entityName.description: A metamodel entity name (the queried/target entity a read starts from, m-case-format `targetEntity`). Matches the `Class` grammar the operation schema's attribute/relationship references use before the dot.

referenceSql.description: Independent naive SQL oracle. Required for non-trivial reads and optional for trivial single-table predicates. A string is dialect-neutral; a dialect-keyed map MUST cover the exact golden SQL dialect set, which the harness verifies.

predicateWrite.description: A predicate-selected set-based write. The instruction, rather than a language method name or golden SQL, is the canonical compile/run input. Assignment list order is data only: descriptor column order determines emitted SET and bind order.

writeTarget.description: The exact entity a predicate-selected write begins from plus its bare m-op-algebra predicate. The harness validates the operation schema and descriptor-dependent scope/modifier rules separately.

writeAssignment.description: One ordered predicate-write assignment. `attr` is a qualified descriptor attribute reference and `value` is a neutral scalar literal; duplicate and unassignable attributes are rejected by model-aware validation.

statementEntry.description: One logical statement in the execution sequence: a closed `{sql, binds}` object. `sql` is a plain string at naive locations (`given.apply`) or a dialect-keyed map (`postgres` / `mariadb`) at golden locations (`then.statements`, per-step `statements`); `binds` is authored once per statement (dialect-agnostic) and defaults to `[]`. The `{sql, binds}` wrapper keeps dialect keys and entry-level keys in separate namespaces, so the schema needs no reserved-key carve-outs. Golden and naive locations narrow `sql` to the map / string form respectively via `goldenStatements` / `naiveStatements`.

binds.description: Bind values for the `?` placeholders in this statement's `sql`. Follows the same scalar-or-dialect-keyed polymorphism as `sql`: a flat ordered array when the bind holes are shared across dialects (the authored default), OR a dialect-keyed map of arrays when the hole structure diverges (e.g. Postgres carries per-segment JSON keys while MariaDB carries one `'$.a.b'` path bind). When a map, its keys MUST equal this entry's `sql` map's keys (harness-asserted). Defaults to empty.

goldenStatements.description: An ordered list of golden statement entries â€” the shared `statementEntry`, with `sql` narrowed to the dialect-keyed map form (dialect texts side by side).

naiveStatements.description: An ordered list of naive statement entries â€” the shared `statementEntry`, with `sql` narrowed to the plain-string form (dialect-agnostic SQL run verbatim on every dialect).

writeComputedMarker.description: A one-key DB-COMPUTED marker in a neutral write row (â‘ ): the attribute's value is derived by the database rather than authored as a literal bind, so the adapter emits the strategy's SQL fragment and the harness cross-check skips the literal bind at this position. This interpretation applies ONLY to a SCALAR ATTRIBUTE column (m-value-object): a value-object column's value is ALWAYS the literal document even when it is marker-SHAPED, so a marker vs a value-object document is disambiguated by the field's declared metamodel role (resolved from `columnOrder(entity)`), NOT by the value's shape.

computed.description: The pk-gen `max` strategy: the column is emitted as `coalesce(max(col), ?) + ?` (its binds are the strategy's coalesce base + increment, NOT an â‘  literal).

increment.description: A self-referential advance emitted as `col = col + ?` (e.g. a sequence registry's `next_val`); the integer is the amount added.

writeRow.description: A flat attribute-named write row â€” the neutral write input (â‘ ), same vocabulary as a fixture row. Reserved control keys are named as explicit `properties`; every OTHER key is an ENTITY ATTRIBUTE name OR a top-level VALUE-OBJECT name (a business/developer name like `id` / `orderedOn` / `address`, NOT a physical column like `ordered_on`). A SCALAR ATTRIBUTE's value is a scalar literal, an explicit `null`, OR a one-key DB-COMPUTED marker object â€” `{ computed: "maxPlusOne" }` or `{ increment: <n> }`; a VALUE-OBJECT field's value is the WHOLE embedded composite document (a JSON object, or a JSON array of objects for a `many` value object), or `null` (m-value-object). The adapter classifies each field by its metamodel role and derives the emitted column list + ORDER from `columnOrder(entity)` â€” never from JSON/YAML key order â€” binding a value object atomically as one document in its columnOrder position. A DB-computed marker vs a value-object document is therefore disambiguated by MODEL ROLE, not by the value's SHAPE: only a scalar-attribute column's value is ever read as a marker, while a value-object column's value is ALWAYS the literal document even when it is marker-SHAPED. A row object therefore CANNOT be `additionalProperties: false`: it names `observedVersion` and admits attribute / value-object keys via `additionalProperties`.

observedVersion.description: The optimistic-lock version the unit of work read (m-opt-lock). A reserved control key, NOT an attribute/column: it is the gate/advance token (the advance `observedVersion + 1` and the `and version = ?` gate are DERIVED, never authored). Present on a versioned update; absent on a versioned insert and on a non-versioned write.

anyOf.description: A value-object document (m-value-object): a value-object-named field binds the WHOLE embedded composite ATOMICALLY as one document value in its columnOrder position â€” a JSON object for a `one` value object, or a JSON array of objects for a `many` one â€” never decomposed into path-level binds. (A null nullable value object uses the scalar `null` branch above and binds SQL NULL.) Whether a marker-SHAPED value (`{computed: â€¦}` / `{increment: n}`) is a DB-computed marker or a value-object document is a MODEL-ROLE decision â€” resolved from `columnOrder(entity)`, NOT from the value's shape â€” that this model-agnostic schema cannot make, so the value branches are NON-exclusive (`anyOf`, no mutual-exclusion guard): a value-object column's value is ALWAYS literal document content, and a marker-shaped value-object document therefore validates via BOTH this branch and the computed-marker branch.

concurrencyStep.description: One node's step within a `when.concurrency` round. Carries its golden SQL as `statements` ({sql, binds} entries, dialect-keyed map form). On the concurrency-SUCCESS shape it additionally declares an explicit `kind`: a `read` step is fetched on its HELD session and its `expectRows` graded; a `write` step is executed and asserts only that it did not block/raise. `kind` is optional in this base def so the error/concurrency shape â€” whose only assertion is the classified error â€” carries no `kind`; the concurrency-success root branch requires it on every present step.

statements.description: This node step's golden SQL, as an ordered list of `{sql, binds}` statement entries (dialect-keyed map form).

kind.description: concurrency-success form only: the EXPLICIT read-vs-write discriminator the runners branch on (replacing the brittle SQL-verb sniffing). `read` â€” the step is fetched on its HELD session (`session.query`) and its rows graded against `expectRows`; `write` â€” the step is executed (`session.execute`) and asserts only that it did not block/raise. Structural: a `read` step MUST carry `expectRows`, a `write` step MUST omit it (the if/then below).

expectRows.description: concurrency-success form only: the rows this node's read MUST return on its HELD session. A read step (`kind: read`) MUST declare it; a write step (`kind: write`) omits it. Absent on the error/concurrency shape, whose only assertion is the classified error.

allOf.description: A read step (`kind: read`) MUST carry the rows it observes on its held session (`expectRows`), which the runner grades.
A write step (`kind: write`) MUST NOT carry `expectRows` â€” it asserts only that it did not block/raise, so there are no rows to grade.
